text: ●       Description: urlscan.io is a web-based service that scans and analyzes websites, providing detailed information about their behavior, content, and potential security threats. It captures screenshots, analyzes HTTP requests, JavaScript execution, and identifies potentially malicious behaviors.
●       Category: Reconnaissance, OSINT (Open Source Intelligence), Threat Intelligence
●       Developer/Maintainer: urlscan.io Ltd (founded by Johannes Gilger)
●       License: Free for basic use; premium subscriptions available for advanced features
As urlscan.io is a web-based service, no traditional installation is required.
○       Web Interface: Simply visit https://urlscan.io in any modern web browser
○       API: Use the REST API for automated scanning and integration with other tools
○       Browser Extension: Available for Chrome and Firefox to quickly scan URLs
○       Create a free account to access additional features and track your scans
○       After creating an account, generate API keys from your account settings
○       API keys are required for programmatic access and to submit private scans
○       Store API keys securely and never share them publicly
○       Free accounts: Limited number of scans per day
○       Premium plans: Higher scan quotas and priority processing
○       Public: Results available to all users
○       Unlisted: Results only accessible via direct URL
○       Private: Results only visible to your account (requires API key)
○       Enter a URL in the search bar and click "Scan"
○       Review the generated report with screenshots and detailed information
○       Search for existing scans by URL, IP, domain, or other indicators
○       Summary: Overview of the scan with key findings
○       HTTP: All HTTP requests made during the visit
○       Console: JavaScript console logs and errors
○       Screenshot: Visual capture of the rendered page
○       Indicators: IPs, domains, and other extracted entities
○       Behaviour: Actions performed by the site's JavaScript
○       Enter a URL in the search box
○       Wait for scan completion and review results
Here’s how the output results look:
Cisco Umbrella rank: 3 (indicating extremely high traffic/popularity)
TLS certificate: Issued by WE2 on March 31st 2025
Google Safe Browsing: No classification for www.google.com
Current DNS A record: 216.58.212.132 (AS15169 - GOOGLE, US)
The site is hosted on Google's infrastructure with IPv6 addressing
The domain has exceptional legitimacy (Cisco Umbrella rank of 3)
Security protocols are properly implemented with valid TLS certification
No malicious classifications from security services
The domain has extensive history (created in 1997) and has been frequently scanned on the platform
The scan confirms this is a legitimate Google website with proper security implementations and no detected threats.
This section of the URLscan.io report provides detailed information about the scanned web page and its technical characteristics.
The URL history section shows the redirect chain and HTTP status codes:
This indicates a standard redirect pattern that first enforces HTTPS encryption, then standardizes to the www subdomain.
The page statistics section provides quantitative metrics about the page load and resources:
HTTPS: 100% (All connections use secure HTTPS protocol)
Countries: 1 (All connections from a single country)
Transfer: 773 kB (Data transferred during page loading)
Cookies: 2 (Number of cookies set by the website)
The site properly implements security best practices with full HTTPS and IPv6 support
Content delivery is distributed across multiple subdomains for performance optimization
The site uses a standard and secure redirect chain to ensure all users access the canonical www.google.com domain over HTTPS
The page is relatively lightweight with modest transfer and cookie usage
Resources are consolidated, loading from only 2 domains which helps limit security exposure
The redirect chain and technical configurations align with security best practices for a high-profile website.

This section of the URLscan.io report provides detailed analysis of the network infrastructure supporting the scanned website, focusing on IP addresses, their geographic locations, and associated network entities.
The table shows the distribution of HTTP requests across Google's IPv6 infrastructure:
All IPs are located in: Germany
All IPs use IPv6 addressing (2a00:1450:4001:xxx::xxxx format)

Load Distribution: The website distributes traffic across multiple IP addresses, with one address (2a00:1450:4001:828::2004) handling the majority (28 of 37) of requests. This suggests a primary server with backup or specialized servers handling specific content.
Infrastructure Consistency: All connections route through Google's autonomous system (AS15169), indicating complete ownership and control of the infrastructure by Google.
Modern Networking: The site uses IPv6 addressing exclusively, demonstrating adoption of modern internet protocols.

Geographic Centralization: All traffic is served from infrastructure located in Germany, likely from a single data center or region, which may be strategically located to serve European users with low latency.
Network Architecture: The IP addressing scheme (all beginning with 2a00:1450:4001) suggests these addresses are part of a contiguous block assigned to Google's European infrastructure.
This pattern is typical for large, sophisticated web services that leverage distributed infrastructure while maintaining centralized control for security and performance optimization.

This section of the URLscan.io report provides an expanded view of the network infrastructure, mapping specific IP addresses to their associated domains and showing the distribution of requests across Google's content delivery network.
First IP shows "1 redirects" indicating it's part of the redirect chain
All IP addresses are located specifically in Frankfurt am Main, Germany
All domains are hosted on Google's infrastructure (ASN15169)
Domain Architecture: The main website (www.google.com) handles most requests (28), while supporting resources are distributed across specialized domains:
Domain Segmentation Strategy: Google separates different types of content across specialized subdomains for:
Security isolation (domain boundaries create security barriers)
Centralized Geographic Infrastructure: All domains resolve to IP addresses in the same location (Frankfurt am Main), suggesting a regional data center serving multiple Google services.
Domain-Specific IP Addressing: Each domain or service type appears to have dedicated IP addresses, indicating sophisticated network segmentation within Google's infrastructure.
Redirect Handling: The initial request to google.com (non-www) redirects to the canonical www.google.com domain, following web best practices.
This detailed mapping demonstrates Google's sophisticated domain architecture that balances centralized infrastructure management with logical separation of services.

This section of the URLscan.io report provides a domain-level view of web traffic, including apex domains, their subdomains, request counts, Cisco Umbrella ranking, and total data transfer. It helps map the communication structure of the scanned website and the associated services or third-party calls.
These subdomains reflect a distribution of functionality:
apis.google.com relates to API calls or service integration
play.google.com connects to Google Play services
These are typically used for serving static resources like fonts, JavaScript, and cached assets, supporting faster load times and performance optimization.

The entry "2" lacks specific domain information in this image, potentially due to rendering issues or grouping behavior in the tool. It may represent aggregated requests not classified under the above apex domains.
Domain Architecture: The traffic distribution illustrates how Google's ecosystem segregates different services by domain:
Specialized APIs and ad services: subdomains like clients6.google.com and apis.google.com
Redirect Chain: The presence of a redirect from google.com to www.google.com reflects standard practice in domain canonicalization to ensure consistency and improve SEO.
Security and Optimization Strategy: Google follows a clear domain segmentation strategy:
Security Isolation: Subdomains are used to enforce browser security boundaries (e.g., Same-Origin Policy).
Performance Optimization: Separating static and dynamic content across domains improves caching and parallel downloads.
Service Separation: Functionality is modularized, reducing interdependency between services.

This section represents the request flow of domains accessed during the scan, showing how each domain was reached and what other domains it triggered. It helps to trace the dependency and origin of third-party requests on the scanned webpage.

The scan shows that google.com initiated a request which was redirected once to www.google.com. This is a common redirection for canonicalization and aligns with best practices to unify domain access.
With 28 requests, it is the dominant origin of activity and also triggered other domains:
It called www.gstatic.com and fonts.gstatic.com, likely for loading static assets (CSS, fonts, or JavaScript libraries).
This points to www.google.com being the main page or frame loaded.
play.google.com – may indicate dynamic links to Google Play or related app content.
ogads-pa.clients6.google.com – likely for ad-serving or telemetry.
apis.google.com – used for script or token-based authentication APIs or SDKs.
This indicates that www.gstatic.com is serving both UI and functional dependencies from related domains.
google.com → www.google.com — This confirms that the base domain redirects users to its 'www' variant for content delivery.
The domain tree follows a structured flow typical of Google-owned service chains:
Ensures modularity by separating static content (gstatic) from dynamic application logic (google.com).
Improves security by sandboxing functionalities via subdomains.
Optimizes performance with content delivery through gstatic.

The Links tab outlines external domains that the scanned webpage links to, either through embedded anchors (<a> tags), JavaScript redirections, or references within the HTML source. This helps in understanding the external navigation or dependencies offered on the page.
Official Google Store – hardware and product sales.
Gmail web client – used for email access.
Google Denmark – localized search engine for Denmark.
Google Account services – authentication and user management.
Primary domain – likely a fallback or homepage link.
Legal documents, privacy policies, and terms of service.
Consent management and cookie handling service.
Help center for user assistance across all Google products.
1. All Links are Internal to Google Ecosystem
All referenced domains belong to Google and serve various functions:
This indicates the scanned page is either a core Google service or a tightly controlled landing page with no third-party links.
The presence of consent.google.com and policies.google.com suggests that the page includes elements related to:
User awareness regarding tracking, privacy settings, and data use
www.google.dk suggests redirection or service availability based on regional domains, pointing toward international user targeting or global service distribution.
The Certs tab displays TLS/SSL certificates used by domains that were contacted or embedded during the page load. These certificates are essential for encrypted communication and domain validation.
Each certificate listed uses a wildcard format (*.), indicating that the certificate is valid for any subdomain under the primary domain (e.g., mail.google.com, play.google.com under *.google.com).
This is common in large organizations to simplify certificate management and secure multiple subdomains with a single certificate.

The "WE2" is likely an internal identifier used by URLscan.io or the certificate authority to label the issuing certificate. Typically, Google certificates are issued by their own Certificate Authority (Google Trust Services) or Let's Encrypt for automation.
All certificates are valid for 3 months, aligning with industry best practices for enhanced security:
Frequent rotation reduces the impact of compromised certificates.
Automates certificate lifecycle via ACME protocol (often using Let's Encrypt).
Each certificate includes a reference to crt.sh, a certificate transparency log viewer, allowing users to verify issuance and public availability for auditing or investigation.

The Frames tab displays embedded HTML <iframe> elements or frame-based content detected during page load. These frames may host third-party content, advertising, trackers, or embedded services, and are important from both a security and content analysis standpoint.
The scan detected only one iframe or top-level frame, corresponding directly to the main page: https://www.google.com/.
This suggests that the page is largely self-contained and not embedding third-party or external services as iframes.
The single frame made 42 HTTP requests, indicating a moderately complex page load with multiple resources such as:
Possibly API calls (e.g., via xhr or fetch)
The Frame ID (F9BF0AE1869C6FA6AA3E1132ECE8B322) is a unique identifier used by URLscan.io to track and reference this frame internally or across reports.
Total HTTP Requests in this Frame: 42
Protocol Used: HTTP/3 (H3) — Google's infrastructure is using QUIC for faster communication.
Latency Range: ~65ms to 214ms — Indicates optimized CDN delivery.
Redirection Chain: HTTP → HTTPS → www.google.com — Ensures encrypted communication.
IP Location (All): Germany — Hosted via Google servers in the EU region.
Total Data Transferred: Significant given script and document payload sizes (document: 260 KB, script: ~997 KB).
All connections are encrypted (HTTPS via HTTP/3), ensuring secure data transfer.
Resource types cover essential web assets: document, styles, fonts, and JavaScript.
No anomalies or suspicious indicators in behavior or verdict sections; all traffic is consistent with standard Google page loading.
1. google.com Requests: Not explicitly shown, but multiple references imply active data handling Cookies:
AEC and __Secure-ENID cookies are set, indicating authentication or tracking mechanisms
Cookie expiry dates are set incorrectly to 1970-01-21, which might suggest either expired cookies or a placeholder used during testing or scraping
Message: "Autofocus processing was blocked because a document already has a focused element."
This indicates a benign browser-side rendering behavior due to DOM handling, not a security issue
34 non-standard global variables identified on the window object
These include functions and objects such as gws_wizbind, WIZ_global_data, googleapis, closure_lm_697634, etc.
Suggests the use of custom JS frameworks and Google's internal libraries for front-end rendering and telemetry
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000 — forces HTTPS for 1 year
X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN — prevents clickjacking by disallowing framing from different origins
X-XSS-Protection: 0 — XSS protection is disabled, likely due to reliance on modern CSPs or browser security updates
Use of Google-owned cookies and global variables confirms interaction with core Google services
JavaScript objects reveal a modular and internal framework for data handling, telemetry, UI behavior, and session control
Security headers indicate industry-standard HTTPS enforcement and framing protection
XSS protection disabled, which is increasingly common among high-profile platforms that rely on more modern mechanisms like Content Security Policy (CSP)
Google splits logic-heavy client-side scripts and telemetry data across different global variables for modular control
Subdomain segmentation and cookie scoping contribute to clear isolation boundaries for authentication, APIs, and static content
These are all external links redirecting to Google-owned domains. Each link is paired with a title and belongs to common Google services or informational resources.
All links are legitimate and part of Google’s ecosystem (no red flags or malicious redirections).
Localized Content: Many URLs contain Danish language parameters (hl=da), indicating the scanned site is tailored for Danish users.
Secure (HTTPS): All links use HTTPS protocol.
UTM Tracking: Several URLs include UTM parameters, suggesting marketing or tracking purposes (e.g., utm_source, utm_campaign).
These links do not pose any direct risk, but:
The presence of multiple tracking parameters (UTM) implies user behavior may be monitored for analytics.
A thorough scan should still validate the integrity of link destinations in case of URL spoofing on non-Google pages that mimic these
The report is divided into four key sections based on structural similarity, IP matching, and domain characteristics.
A large volume of requests has been recorded for structurally similar URLs under the www.google.com domain. Key details:
Observation: Multiple repetitive hits across the root and minimal paths suggest crawler activity or automated probes on Google's landing and error pages.
2. 10,000+ Hits for Same ASN but Different Domains
These are URLs hosted under the same ASN (Autonomous System Number) but not google.com. Many are blogspot.com and forms/docs subdomains:

Observation: Heavy interaction with Google services (Docs, YouTube, Accounts) and blogspot blogs, some of which may be spam or content-farming domains. URLs such as bgmiluckyspin09.blogspot.com suggest gambling or promotional campaigns.
3. 66 Hits for the Same IP but Different Domain
These hits originate from shared IPs but target non-Google domains:
Observation: Static asset delivery domains (gstatic.com) and a partner page under google.com. This section indicates shared infrastructure or CDN usage, mostly hosted in Germany.
4. 0 Hits for the Exact Same Submitted URL
No identical matches were found for the specific submitted URL. This means while many structurally similar pages exist, this exact URL has not been previously indexed or scanned.
No direct malicious behavior detected in the links, but some blogspot and third-party domains warrant further validation.
Repeated paths to Google services could indicate automated scanning or bot traffic.
Use of static content (gstatic.com) is normal, but monitoring is advisable when paired with blogspot domains—often used in phishing and scam campaigns.
Same ASN but suspicious TLDs (e.g., blogspot.com spam clusters) should be flagged for deeper inspection.
Displays the rendered structure of the HTML page as seen by a browser.
Helps detect malicious scripts, hidden elements, or dynamic modifications injected via JavaScript.
Useful for analyzing client-side behaviors (e.g., script injections, iframe insertions, clickjacking).
Shows the raw HTML, JavaScript, and media delivered by the server.
Focuses on static resources and original payloads.
Good for identifying hardcoded malicious links, obfuscated code, and suspicious file inclusions.
Details the API requests and responses made by the page.
Includes AJAX/XHR traffic, RESTful endpoints, and sometimes GraphQL queries.
Critical for analyzing data exfiltration, command & control (C2) traffic, and backend interaction patterns.
■       Solution: Wait for rate limit to reset or upgrade to a premium account
■       Solution: Large or complex sites may take longer to scan; be patient or try scanning specific pages
■       Solution: Check if the site blocks automated scanners or requires authentication
■       Solution: Verify your API key is valid and has sufficient permissions
○       Public scans are visible to everyone, including potential targets
○       Your IP address is visible to urlscan.io when submitting scans
○       Submitted URLs become part of the urlscan.io database
○       Use private scan mode for sensitive investigations
○       Be aware that site owners may be able to see that their site was scanned
○       Do not use urlscan.io for unauthorized security testing
